content:
1. Introduction
Although two-thirds of the Earth's surface is covered by water, less than 3% is freshwater. Freshwater scarcity is becoming an urgent global concern, and an ever-growing proportion of these limited resources are rendered unusable by industrial effluents, agricultural runoff, and emerging micropollutants. In 2022, according to the WHO, only 73% of the global population (6 billion people) accessed and used safely managed drinking-water services, with 2.2 billion people without access to clean water remaining [1]. To mitigate these effects, conventional water-treatment processes that combine physical, chemical, and biological steps, which typically rely on a combination of mechanical separation, chemical dosing, and biological degradation across multistage treatment facilities, have been implemented. However, these processes are often unprepared to address the growing presence of persistent pollutants and often fail to remove all classes of contaminants. Additionally, these processes can generate secondary pollutants or toxic side products while also requiring specific infrastructure and expertise, remaining energy- and capital-intensive [2,3]. To address these issues further, membrane separation technology has garnered attention in recent decades from both scientific and industrial communities because of its attributes, such as high separation efficiency, high selectivity and permeability, and low investment and maintenance costs [4–7].

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content: Membranes can be broadly categorized into isotropic membranes, which are uniform in composition and physical structure, and anisotropic membranes, which consist of multiple layers with distinct properties and compositions [8]. Pressure-driven membrane processes such as microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, and reverse osmosis (RO) are widely employed and are typically fabricated from synthetic polymers such as polyethylene, polypropylene, polytetrafluoroethylene, and cellulose acetate. Each class targets different size ranges of contaminants: microfiltration (0.1–10 μm) and ultrafiltration (0.01–0.1 μm) are effective against larger particulates, bacteria, and viruses, whereas nanofiltration and RO, with pore sizes in the range of 0.5–2 nm and below 0.5 nm, respectively, can remove dissolved salts and small organic molecules. Rejection and permeability rates are the most critical distinguishing features among different types of membranes [9–11]. For decades, polymers and ceramics have been the primary materials used for membrane production. However, polymer membranes have limitations, such as the trade-off between permeability and selectivity, being nonbiodegradable and prone to fouling [12,13]. Ceramics have shown outstanding mechanical, thermal, and chemical characteristics but have high production costs [14,15]. Therefore, it is critical to develop nanofiltration membranes capable of mitigating these issues, and it is essential that they exhibit mechanical and chemical stability, high durability, high liquid permeance, and excellent separation efficiency while keeping the fabrication process simple, scalable and affordable. The integration of antibacterial properties is also crucial, as biofouling remains a significant barrier to long-term and practical membrane operation [16].

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content: To overcome such limitations, 2D materials, such as graphene, graphene oxide (GO), and transitional metal carbides and nitrides, known as MXenes, have shown great potential for membrane separation applications [17]. Owing to their atomic thickness and micrometer lateral size, these materials exhibit great versatility and separation behaviors while also minimizing mass transport resistance and achieving high permeance flux. Among these materials, transitional metal carbides (Ti3C2Tx) have attracted particular attention because of their hydrophilic nature (attributed to surface termination groups such as =O or –OH), high water permeability, stable physical and chemical properties and ease of functionalization [18–20]. Ti3C2Tx can also act as an effective antimicrobial agent. The atomically thin nanosheet structure presents abundant sharp edges that can directly pierce and destabilize bacterial cell envelopes, known as the nanoknife effect [21]. Furthermore, hydrophilic surface terminations endow bacterial cells with strong affinity, facilitating contact and thereby amplifying this mechanism. Finally, Ti3C2Tx exhibited minimal biofilm formation (“antibiofouling”) when incorporated into water purification membranes, combining rapid bactericidal action with sustained antifouling performance. These antibacterial properties are essential for ensuring long-term membrane operation in water treatment applications [22,23]. Previous studies on other 2D-based membranes have shown that ion rejection relies on two dominant mechanisms: size exclusion—due to the rigid ionic hydration shell in narrow channels—and electrostatic repulsion—due to the surface charge of the membrane. MXenes show exceptional ion sieving capabilities, attributed to their negatively charged and nanometer-sized interlayer channels. These features facilitate ultrafast water transport while effectively rejecting selected ions [19,20,24].

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content: Laminar MXene membranes, assembled through vacuum-assisted filtration, have demonstrated highly efficient, charge- and size-selective ion separation while enabling ultrafast water permeation [25]. It was recently highlighted that the performance of these MXene membranes is not solely determined by their intrinsic chemistry but also strongly influenced by other factors, such as flake size, interlayer spacing, membrane architecture and microstructure [26]. Other reported ultrathin Ti3C2Tx laminates showed marked size exclusion properties and exploited surface charge to drive electrokinetic ion transport under applied pressure gradients, generating measurable streaming currents and pointing toward integrated separation and energy harvesting devices [27–30]. MXenes also offer great potential for functionalization owing to their surface functional groups, which make them highly tunable [31]. Specific modifications (e.g., covalent and electrostatic bridging of the flakes) can increase salt rejection, water permeability, and nanochannel stability [22]. MXene membranes incorporating Prussian blue intercalants under ambient conditions enabled controlled tuning of the nanochannel spacing, improving both water permeance and salt rejection [32]. Similarly, MXene membranes assembled on anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) supports employed Fe(OH)3 nanoparticles as sacrificial intercalants to temporarily expand the nanochannels, which were subsequently removed to yield enlarged transport pathways. In that work, the membranes achieved high permeability values (>1000 L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹) while maintaining high rejection rates (>90%) for molecules larger than 2.5 nm [33].

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content: When employing MXene as a base material for water separation membranes, its susceptibility to oxidation in air and water must be considered, particularly at the edges and defect sites of the flakes [34]. To address this limitation, stabilization approaches such as edge-capping have been developed. For example, capping MXene nanosheets with sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP) resulted in membranes with increased stability in aqueous environments and consistent separation performance compared to pristine MXene. The intercalation of Ti3C2Tx MXene with cations’ salts (e.g., Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Al³⁺) has also been demonstrated to suppress swelling and enhance salt rejection capabilities [35–37]. Beyond salt removal, MXene-based membranes have also achieved significant advances in broader water-purification applications. Hybrid membranes incorporating supramolecular cyclodextrins on the MXene surface have been used to optimize nanofiltration structures, simultaneously enhancing water permeance and dye rejection [38]. Membranes fabricated from pillararene-intercalated MXene nanosheets with an interlayer spacing of 1.36 nm enabled efficient purification of antibiotic-containing water and exhibited permeance values up to two orders of magnitude higher than most polymeric nanofiltration membranes [39]. Furthermore, pillararene-intercalated MXene hybrid membranes have demonstrated voltage-gated tuning of selective transport pathways, providing novel perspectives on water treatment solutions [40]. Overall, MXene laminar membranes have demonstrated simultaneously high flux and selectivity, alongside high tunability, presenting great promise for water separation applications [22,41].

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content: Liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) allows the production of 2D materials, such as MXenes, in large quantities with high yields, showing high potential for scalable mass production [42–44]. LPE is the most common way to obtain MXene flakes [19]. Typically, Ti3C2Tx MXene is obtained from the etching of MAX phases (in this case, Ti3AlC2), where bonded Al layers in the MAX phase are selectively etched by chemical reactions with strong acids. Following Al removal, MXenes are usually in the form of multilayered flakes, and delamination occurs in water or other organic solvents, often assisted by sonication [45]. The choice of solvent is critical, especially in the case of Ti3C2Tx MXene, owing to its degradation and oxidation in oxygen-rich solvents. Conventional solvents such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and dimethylformamide (DMF) often present safety concerns due to their toxicity. As a bioderived alternative, dihydrolevoglucosenone (Cyrene) has been employed for the LPE of Ti3C2Tx MXene and can improve dispersion stability and suppress oxidation [44]. Here we use Cyrene-processed Ti3C2Tx dispersions as building blocks for the fabrication of ion-sieving and antibacterial membranes. Ion transport and separation studies were conducted to assess the sieving properties of the membranes, while bacterial tests with Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were used to evaluate antibacterial and antibiofouling behavior.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
2. Experimental
2.1. Materials
Titanium aluminum carbide (MAX phase) powder (Ti3AlC2, purity: 99%, average particle size 40–60 μm) was purchased from Nanoshel-UK Ltd. Dihydrolevoglucosenone (Cyrene) and hydrofluoric acid (HF, 48%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Dispersing additives BYK-2012 and BYK-190 were supplied by BYK (a member of ALTANA). Hydrophilic polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes (47 mm diameter, 100 nm) and anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) membranes (47 mm diameter, 20 nm) were purchased from Durapore–Merck Millipore Ltd. All materials were used as received.

2.2. HF treatment of the MAX phase
Following all relevant safety measures [44], 50 mg mL⁻¹ MAX phase powder was progressively added to a 10% aqueous hydrofluoric acid solution and continuously stirred for 18 h at room temperature. The resulting dark solution was washed numerous times until the pH reached 6 via a centrifuge (3500 rpm for 5 min in each stage, repeated). The resulting solution was vacuum filtered through a PTFE membrane with a pore size of 220 nm and a diameter of 47 mm to obtain a powder known as the MXene multilayer.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
2.3. Preparation of few-layer MXenes
Five grams of the MXene multilayer powder was added to 250 mL of Cyrene, in which 0.5 v% of the additives BYK-190 and BYK-2012 were added. The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min to achieve a uniform dispersion. The dispersion was exfoliated via tip sonication (Branson Digital Sonifier SFX 550, 0.55 kW of power at a frequency of 20 kHz for high-volume processing, a 1/2″ disruptor horn with a 1/2″ extension-type tip) at a 45% amplitude and an ON:OFF ratio of 10:5. Exfoliation was performed under ambient laboratory conditions with the beaker placed in an ice-water bath to dissipate heat generated during sonication, for a total duration of 120 min. The resulting black dispersion was collected and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min to remove thick and unexfoliated material. The supernatant containing the few-layer MXene nanosheets was then collected.

2.4. Membrane preparation
MXene membranes were prepared by vacuum filtration of 5 mL of the dispersion onto PVDF supports with a 100 nm pore size. After the entire volume was filtered and the MXene laminate formed, 2-propanol (IPA) was added to the filtration flask to remove any residual solvent and additives. For comparison purposes in bacterial adhesion studies, MXene membranes were also assembled on AAO supports.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
2.5. Sample characterization
Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was used to analyze the Ti3C2Tx flakes and membrane surface topography. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was employed to study surface and cross-sectional morphology, as well as bacterial adhesion. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigated the structure of MXene flakes. X-ray diffraction (XRD) tracked structural changes across the MAX precursor, etched bulk, exfoliated nanosheets, and final MXene laminate. Raman spectroscopy and UV–Vis spectrophotometry characterized the MXene. Electrokinetic measurements determined the zeta potential as a function of pH. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed surface termination groups.

2.6. Ion transport studies
Ion transport was studied using a custom-made tau cell. MXene membranes were sealed on an acrylic sheet with an opening of 2×2 mm². Two reservoirs (feed and permeate) were separated by the membrane. For diffusion potential measurements, a concentration ratio of 100 (1 M | 10 mM) was used for KCl, CaCl₂, and AlCl₃ solutions. I–V characteristics were recorded, and diffusion potentials were calculated. For salt rejection, forward osmosis (FO) experiments were performed: one side filled with 1 M salt solution (KCl, NaCl, CaCl₂, or AlCl₃), the other with water. After 12 h, concentrations were measured with a TDS meter, and rejection was calculated.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
2.7. Bacterial adhesion studies
Escherichia coli (E. coli, CECT 434) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, CECT 435) were cultured overnight in tryptic soy broth at 37 °C. Bacterial concentration was adjusted to 10⁶ CFU mL⁻¹. Sterilized samples (≈60 mm²) were incubated with 300 μL of bacterial suspension at 37 °C for 24 h. After washing with phosphate-buffered saline, bacteria were detached by sonication and vortexing. Serial dilutions were plated onto tryptic soy agar, incubated overnight, and colony-forming units were counted. Antibacterial activity was calculated relative to control membranes (PVDF and AAO without MXene).

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Characterization of MXene dispersions and membranes
Multilayered Ti3AlC2 was exfoliated into atomic-thick MXene flakes in Cyrene, resulting in a homogeneous dispersion as confirmed by the Tyndall effect. UV–Vis absorption showed a peak at ~820 nm, typical of exfoliated Ti3C2Tx nanosheets. AFM revealed sub-μm lateral size and multilayered nature, while TEM showed polygonal flakes with sharp edges. Vacuum-filtered MXene membranes on PVDF supports were flexible and robust. SEM showed randomly oriented MXene flakes forming an interconnected, defect-free surface and a compact stacked morphology in cross-section. XRD tracked structural changes: the MAX phase showed sharp (00l) reflections; after etching, only basal (00l) reflections persisted with increased interlayer spacing; exfoliated nanosheets showed a (002) peak at 8.9° (d≈9.9 Å); the final laminate had an additional reflection at 2θ≈6.5° (d=13.6 Å) due to partial restacking stabilized by intercalated water and surface terminations. Raman spectroscopy confirmed characteristic Eg and A1g modes of Ti3C2Tx. Contact angle measurements gave ~65°, consistent with literature. Zeta potential was negative across pH 3–11, reaching ~ –20 mV at alkaline pH, indicating lower surface charge density compared to water-dispersed flakes. XPS confirmed the presence of Ti–C bonds and surface terminations (–F, –O, –OH).

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
3.2. Ion transport and selectivity
Ion conductance increased with concentration for KCl, CaCl₂, and AlCl₃, despite differences in cation hydration diameters (K⁺ 6.6 Å, Ca²⁺ 8.4 Å, Al³⁺ 9.6 Å). The interlayer spacing (13.6 Å) allows hydrated ions to enter with reduced steric hindrance. However, the theoretical van der Waals gap (3.8 Å) would only allow water molecules to pass, implying that ion permeation occurs through tortuous pathways formed by disordered stacking of flakes, as supported by TEM and soaking stability tests (no swelling after 300 h in water). Under a 100-fold concentration gradient, diffusion potentials were negative for all salts, indicating anion-dominated transport. The magnitude increased with cation valence: ≈ –40 mV (KCl), –75 mV (CaCl₂), –80 mV (AlCl₃), showing that Cl⁻ moves more freely while cation mobility is increasingly suppressed. Ion selectivity from the Nernst equation increased with cation hydration size, and the cation-to-anion mobility ratio (from the Henderson equation) decreased from ≈0.50 (KCl) to 0.30 (AlCl₃), confirming anion selectivity despite the negative zeta potential. This non-Donnan behavior arises from weak confinement, strong interactions between multivalent cations and surface terminations, and hydration asymmetry.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
3.3. Salt rejection in forward osmosis
Forward osmosis experiments showed rejection rates above 92% for all tested salts: KCl (92%), NaCl (95%), CaCl₂ (97%), AlCl₃ (97%). Rejection increased with hydrated cation diameter, consistent with size exclusion and strong intercalation/interaction of larger multivalent cations with the MXene surface. The results confirm that size exclusion and cation–surface interactions dominate salt separation, overriding the weak electrostatic non-Donnan preference observed in diffusion potential measurements. Comparison with literature shows that the Cyrene-processed Ti3C2Tx/PVDF membrane achieves competitive salt rejection, particularly for multivalent ions, and demonstrates the importance of membrane design (cross-linking, composites) for stabilizing nanochannels and enhancing surface charge density.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
3.4. Antibacterial and biofouling resistance
Antibacterial tests on PVDF and AAO supports showed that MXene-coated membranes reduced bacterial adhesion compared to controls. For MXene/PVDF, S. aureus adhesion decreased by 54% and E. coli by 29%. MXene/AAO gave even stronger reduction: 82% for S. aureus and 62% for E. coli. Greater adhesion of E. coli was observed across all membranes, attributed to the stiffer outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria resisting the nanoknife effect of MXene flakes. The smoother AAO support (roughness ~18 nm) showed better antibacterial performance than the rougher PVDF support (~26 nm), indicating that lower roughness maximizes direct contact between bacterial cell walls and nanosheet edges, enhancing physical disruption. The antibacterial mechanism involves physical membrane disruption (nanoknife effect) and generation of reactive oxygen species. These results demonstrate that MXene membranes offer significant biofouling resistance, with surface roughness playing a key role.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification

content:
4. Conclusions
Ti3C2Tx MXene dispersions were produced via liquid-phase exfoliation in Cyrene, a bioderived solvent that improves handling, reduces oxidation, and yields stable dispersions. Cyrene-processed Ti3C2Tx dispersions were used to fabricate ion-sieving and antibacterial laminates via vacuum filtration on PVDF supports. The resulting membranes exhibited a compact, defect-free lamellar structure with an interlayer spacing of 13.6 Å, and water soaking tests confirmed structural stability. Ion transport studies revealed anion-selective behavior despite a negative zeta potential, attributed to a combination of membrane architecture, interlayer expansion in aqueous electrolytes, and surface charge modulation. Salt rejection increased with hydrated cation diameter (KCl < NaCl < CaCl₂ < AlCl₃), reaching up to 97% for multivalent salts, consistent with size exclusion and strong cation–surface interactions. Antibacterial tests showed significant biofouling resistance, with smoother AAO-supported laminates outperforming rougher PVDF-supported ones, indicating that lower surface roughness enhances the nanoknife effect. This work demonstrates that beyond surface charge, membrane performance depends critically on nanosheet configuration and microstructure. While Cyrene replaces toxic solvents, the synthesis still relies on HF etching; future work should integrate greener fluorine-free etching methods for fully sustainable MXene membranes.

citation.title: Sustainable MXene membranes for ion sieving and antibacterial water purification
